solution:
Solution 1. Subtract the first row from each of the other rows, to get
\[
D_{n}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
-2 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & n
\end{array}\right)
\]

Then for \( 2 \leq i \leq n-1 \), add \( 2 /(i+1) \) times the \( i \) th column to the first column to obtain
\[
D_{n}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{4}+\cdots+\frac{2}{n} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & n
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The resulting matrix is upper triangular, so the determinant is the product of the diagonal elements, which is
\[
n!\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{n}\right)
\]

Solution 2 (J.P. Grossman). We derive a recursion for \( D_{n+1} \). Expand (1) along the last row to get \( D_{n}=n D_{n-1}+(n-1) \) !. Divide by \( n \) ! to obtain
\[
\frac{D_{n}}{n!}=\frac{D_{n-1}}{(n-1)!}+\frac{1}{n}
\]
from which the result follows.
Solution 3. Define
\[
D_{n+1}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}\right)=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
1+a_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1+a_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1+a_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1+a_{4} & \cdots & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1+a_{n}
\end{array}\right)
\]

The problem asks for \( D_{n}(2,3, \ldots, n) \). We will prove the identity
\[
D_{n+1}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}\right)=\prod_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}+\sum_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{\substack{j=1 \\ j \neq i}}^{n} a_{j}
\]

This formula follows immediately from the recursion
\[
D_{n+1}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}\right)=a_{n} D_{n}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n-1}\right)+a_{n-1} D_{n}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n-2}, 0\right)
\]

To prove this recursion, subtract the \( (n-1) \) st column from the \( n \)th column, and then expand along the \( n \)th column.

If all the \( a_{i} \) are nonzero, we can write the polynomial \( D_{n}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n-1}\right) \) in the form
\[
D_{n}\left(a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n-1}\right)=a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{n-1}\left(1+\frac{1}{a_{1}}+\frac{1}{a_{2}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{a_{n-1}}\right)
\]

This problem is the special case \( a_{i}=i+1 \).
Remark. This formula can also be used for a generalization of Problem 1993B5; see page 188.

Solution 4. The following result is Problem 7 of Part VII of [PS], and appeared as Problem 1978A2 [PutnamII, p. 31].

Let \( a, b, p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{n} \) be real numbers with \( a \neq b \). Define
\[
f(x)=\left(p_{1}-x\right)\left(p_{2}-x\right)\left(p_{3}-x\right) \cdots\left(p_{n}-x\right)
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
p_{1} & a & a & a & \cdots & a & a \\
b & p_{2} & a & a & \cdots & a & a \\
b & b & p_{3} & a & \cdots & a & a \\
b & b & b & p_{4} & \cdots & a & a \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
b & b & b & b & \cdots & p_{n-1} & a \\
b & b & b & b & \cdots & b & p_{n}
\end{array}\right)=\frac{b f(a)-a f(b)}{b-a}
\]

The left side of (3) is a polynomial in \( b \), and hence continuous in \( b \). Letting \( a=1 \), \( b \rightarrow 1 \), and using L'Hopital's Rule, we find
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
p_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & p_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & p_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & p_{4} & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & p_{n-1} & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & p_{n}
\end{array}\right)=f(1)-f^{\prime}(1)
\]

Letting \( p_{i}=a_{i}+1 \), we recover (2), using for example equation (1) on page 136.

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. Subtract the first row from each of the other rows to get
\[
\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
-2 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & \text{sizevar}
\end{array}\right).
\]

Then for $2\leq \text{indexone}\leq \text{sizevar}-1$ add $2/(\text{indexone}+1)$ times the $\text{indexone}$th column to the first column to obtain
\[
\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{4}+\cdots+\frac{2}{\text{sizevar}} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & \text{sizevar}
\end{array}\right).
\]
The resulting matrix is upper-triangular, so the determinant is the product of the diagonal entries,
\[
\text{sizevar}!\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\text{sizevar}}\right).
\]

Solution 2 (J.P. Grossman). We derive a recursion for $\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}+1}$. Expanding the original matrix along the last row gives
\[\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}=\text{sizevar}\,\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}-1}+(\text{sizevar}-1)!.\]
Dividing by $\text{sizevar}!$ yields
\[
\frac{\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}}{\text{sizevar}!}=\frac{\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}-1}}{(\text{sizevar}-1)!}+\frac{1}{\text{sizevar}},
\]
from which the claimed result follows by induction.

Solution 3. Define
\[
\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}+1}(\rowshift_{1},\ldots,\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}})=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
1+\rowshift_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1+\rowshift_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1+\rowshift_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1+\rowshift_{4} & \cdots & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1+\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}}
\end{array}\right).
\]
The problem asks for $\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}(2,3,\ldots,\text{sizevar})$. We prove the identity
\[
\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}+1}(\rowshift_{1},\ldots,\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}})=\prod_{\text{indexone}=1}^{\text{sizevar}}\rowshift_{\text{indexone}}+\sum_{\text{indexone}=1}^{\text{sizevar}}\prod_{\substack{\indextwo=1\\ \indextwo\neq\text{indexone}}}^{\text{sizevar}}\rowshift_{\indextwo}.
\]
This follows from the recursion
\[
\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}+1}(\rowshift_{1},\ldots,\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}})=\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}}\,\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}(\rowshift_{1},\ldots,\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}-1})+\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}-1}\,\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}(\rowshift_{1},\ldots,\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}-2},0),
\]
obtained by subtracting the $(\text{sizevar}-1)$st column from the $\text{sizevar}$th and expanding along the new $\text{sizevar}$th column.

If all $\rowshift_{\text{indexone}}\neq0$, we can write
\[
\text{determinseq}_{\text{sizevar}}(\rowshift_{1},\ldots,\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}-1})=\rowshift_{1}\rowshift_{2}\cdots\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}-1}\left(1+\frac{1}{\rowshift_{1}}+\frac{1}{\rowshift_{2}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\rowshift_{\text{sizevar}-1}}\right).
\]
The original problem is the special case $\rowshift_{\text{indexone}}=\text{indexone}+1$.

Remark. This formula also applies to a generalization of Problem 1993B5; see page 188.

Solution 4. The following result is Problem 7 of Part VII of [PS] and appeared as Problem 1978A2 [PutnamII, p. 31].

Let $\constalpha,\constbeta,\paramroot_{1},\paramroot_{2},\ldots,\paramroot_{\text{sizevar}}$ be real numbers with $\constalpha\neq\constbeta$. Define
\[
\polyfunc(\varpoint)=(\paramroot_{1}-\varpoint)(\paramroot_{2}-\varpoint)\cdots(\paramroot_{\text{sizevar}}-\varpoint).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
\paramroot_{1} & \constalpha & \constalpha & \constalpha & \cdots & \constalpha & \constalpha \\
\constbeta & \paramroot_{2} & \constalpha & \constalpha & \cdots & \constalpha & \constalpha \\
\constbeta & \constbeta & \paramroot_{3} & \constalpha & \cdots & \constalpha & \constalpha \\
\constbeta & \constbeta & \constbeta & \paramroot_{4} & \cdots & \constalpha & \constalpha \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
\constbeta & \constbeta & \constbeta & \constbeta & \cdots & \paramroot_{\text{sizevar}-1} & \constalpha \\
\constbeta & \constbeta & \constbeta & \constbeta & \cdots & \constbeta & \paramroot_{\text{sizevar}}
\end{array}\right)=\frac{\constbeta\,\polyfunc(\constalpha)-\constalpha\,\polyfunc(\constbeta)}{\constbeta-\constalpha}.
\]
The left-hand side is a polynomial in $\constbeta$ and hence continuous in $\constbeta$. Letting $\constalpha=1$, $\constbeta\to1$ and using L'Hopital's rule we obtain
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
\paramroot_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & \paramroot_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & \paramroot_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & \paramroot_{4} & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & \paramroot_{\text{sizevar}-1} & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & \paramroot_{\text{sizevar}}
\end{array}\right)=\polyfunc(1)-\polyfunc^{\prime}(1).
\]
Finally, setting $\paramroot_{\text{indexone}}=\rowshift_{\text{indexone}}+1$ recovers the formula established in Solution 3 (see, for example, equation (1) on page 136).

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. Subtract the first row from each of the other rows, to get
\[
sequoia=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
-2 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & galaxytime
\end{array}\right)
\]

Then for \( 2 \leq lampflower \leq galaxytime-1 \), add \( 2 /(lampflower+1) \) times the \( lampflower \) th column to the first column to obtain
\[
sequoia=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{4}+\cdots+\frac{2}{galaxytime} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & galaxytime
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The resulting matrix is upper triangular, so the determinant is the product of the diagonal elements, which is
\[
galaxytime!\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{galaxytime}\right)
\]

Solution 2 (J.P. Grossman). We derive a recursion for \( sequoia_{galaxytime+1} \). Expand (1) along the last row to get \( sequoia=galaxytime \, sequoia_{galaxytime-1}+(galaxytime-1) !\). Divide by \( galaxytime ! \) to obtain
\[
\frac{sequoia}{galaxytime!}=\frac{sequoia_{galaxytime-1}}{(galaxytime-1)!}+\frac{1}{galaxytime}
\]
from which the result follows.

Solution 3. Define
\[
sequoia_{galaxytime+1}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime}\right)=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
1+ribbonfield_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1+ribbonfield_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1+ribbonfield_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1+ribbonfield_{4} & \cdots & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1+ribbonfield_{galaxytime}
\end{array}\right)
\]

The problem asks for \( sequoia_{galaxytime}(2,3, \ldots, galaxytime) \). We will prove the identity
\[
sequoia_{galaxytime+1}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime}\right)=\prod_{lampflower=1}^{galaxytime} ribbonfield_{lampflower}+\sum_{lampflower=1}^{galaxytime} \prod_{\substack{stormmirror=1 \\ stormmirror \neq lampflower}}^{galaxytime} ribbonfield_{stormmirror}
\]

This formula follows immediately from the recursion
\[
sequoia_{galaxytime+1}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime}\right)=ribbonfield_{galaxytime} \, sequoia_{galaxytime}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime-1}\right)+ribbonfield_{galaxytime-1} \, sequoia_{galaxytime}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime-2}, 0\right)
\]

To prove this recursion, subtract the \( (galaxytime-1) \) st column from the \( galaxytime \)th column, and then expand along the \( galaxytime \)th column.

If all the \( ribbonfield_{lampflower} \) are nonzero, we can write the polynomial \( sequoia_{galaxytime}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime-1}\right) \) in the form
\[
sequoia_{galaxytime}\left(ribbonfield_{1}, \ldots, ribbonfield_{galaxytime-1}\right)=ribbonfield_{1} ribbonfield_{2} \cdots ribbonfield_{galaxytime-1}\left(1+\frac{1}{ribbonfield_{1}}+\frac{1}{ribbonfield_{2}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{ribbonfield_{galaxytime-1}}\right)
\]

This problem is the special case \( ribbonfield_{lampflower}=lampflower+1 \).
Remark. This formula can also be used for a generalization of Problem 1993B5; see page 188.

Solution 4. The following result is Problem 7 of Part VII of [PS], and appeared as Problem 1978A2 [PutnamII, p. 31].

Let \( quartzleaf, tigerwindow, candlesky_{1}, candlesky_{2}, \ldots, candlesky_{galaxytime} \) be real numbers with \( quartzleaf \neq tigerwindow \). Define
\[
ottershade(pebbleworld)=\left(candlesky_{1}-pebbleworld\right)\left(candlesky_{2}-pebbleworld\right)\left(candlesky_{3}-pebbleworld\right) \cdots\left(candlesky_{galaxytime}-pebbleworld\right)
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
candlesky_{1} & quartzleaf & quartzleaf & quartzleaf & \cdots & quartzleaf & quartzleaf \\
tigerwindow & candlesky_{2} & quartzleaf & quartzleaf & \cdots & quartzleaf & quartzleaf \\
tigerwindow & tigerwindow & candlesky_{3} & quartzleaf & \cdots & quartzleaf & quartzleaf \\
tigerwindow & tigerwindow & tigerwindow & candlesky_{4} & \cdots & quartzleaf & quartzleaf \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
tigerwindow & tigerwindow & tigerwindow & tigerwindow & \cdots & candlesky_{galaxytime-1} & quartzleaf \\
tigerwindow & tigerwindow & tigerwindow & tigerwindow & \cdots & tigerwindow & candlesky_{galaxytime}
\end{array}\right)=\frac{tigerwindow \, ottershade(quartzleaf)-quartzleaf \, ottershade(tigerwindow)}{tigerwindow-quartzleaf}
\]

The left side of (3) is a polynomial in \( tigerwindow \), and hence continuous in \( tigerwindow \). Letting \( quartzleaf=1 \), \( tigerwindow \rightarrow 1 \), and using L'Hopital's Rule, we find
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
candlesky_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & candlesky_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & candlesky_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & candlesky_{4} & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & candlesky_{galaxytime-1} & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & candlesky_{galaxytime}
\end{array}\right)=ottershade(1)-ottershade^{\prime}(1)
\]

Letting \( candlesky_{lampflower}=ribbonfield_{lampflower}+1 \), we recover (2), using for example equation (1) on page 136.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. Subtract the first row from each of the other rows, to get
\[
indeterminate_{infinitecount}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
-2 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & infinitecount
\end{array}\right)
\]

Then for $ 2 \le fixedindex \le infinitecount-1 $, add $ 2 /(fixedindex+1) $ times the $ fixedindex $ th column to the first column to obtain
\[
indeterminate_{infinitecount}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{4}+\cdots+\frac{2}{infinitecount} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & infinitecount
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The resulting matrix is upper triangular, so the determinant is the product of the diagonal elements, which is
\[
infinitecount!\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{infinitecount}\right)
\]

Solution 2 (J.P. Grossman). We derive a recursion for $ indeterminate_{infinitecount+1} $. Expand (1) along the last row to get $ indeterminate_{infinitecount}=infinitecount\, indeterminate_{infinitecount-1}+(infinitecount-1)! $. Divide by $ infinitecount! $ to obtain
\[
\frac{indeterminate_{infinitecount}}{infinitecount!}=\frac{indeterminate_{infinitecount-1}}{(infinitecount-1)!}+\frac{1}{infinitecount}
\]
from which the result follows.

Solution 3. Define
\[
indeterminate_{infinitecount+1}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount})=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
1+zeroparam_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1+zeroparam_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1+zeroparam_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1+zeroparam_{4} & \cdots & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1+zeroparam_{infinitecount}
\end{array}\right)
\]

The problem asks for $ indeterminate_{infinitecount}(2,3, \ldots, infinitecount) $. We will prove the identity
\[
indeterminate_{infinitecount+1}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount})=\prod_{fixedindex=1}^{infinitecount} zeroparam_{fixedindex}+\sum_{fixedindex=1}^{infinitecount} \prod_{\substack{steadyindex=1 \\ steadyindex \neq fixedindex}}^{infinitecount} zeroparam_{steadyindex}
\]

This formula follows immediately from the recursion
\[
indeterminate_{infinitecount+1}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount})=zeroparam_{infinitecount}\, indeterminate_{infinitecount}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount-1})+zeroparam_{infinitecount-1}\, indeterminate_{infinitecount}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount-2}, 0)
\]

To prove this recursion, subtract the $(infinitecount-1)$ st column from the $ infinitecount $th column, and then expand along the $ infinitecount $th column.

If all the $ zeroparam_{fixedindex} $ are nonzero, we can write the polynomial $ indeterminate_{infinitecount}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount-1}) $ in the form
\[
indeterminate_{infinitecount}(zeroparam_{1}, \ldots, zeroparam_{infinitecount-1})=zeroparam_{1} zeroparam_{2} \cdots zeroparam_{infinitecount-1}\left(1+\frac{1}{zeroparam_{1}}+\frac{1}{zeroparam_{2}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{zeroparam_{infinitecount-1}}\right)
\]

This problem is the special case $ zeroparam_{fixedindex}=fixedindex+1 $.

Remark. This formula can also be used for a generalization of Problem 1993B5; see page 188.

Solution 4. The following result is Problem 7 of Part VII of [PS], and appeared as Problem 1978A2 [PutnamII, p. 31].

Let $ endpointval, startpoint, norootval_{1}, norootval_{2}, \ldots, norootval_{infinitecount} $ be real numbers with $ endpointval \neq startpoint $. Define
\[
constantfn(constantval)=\left(norootval_{1}-constantval\right)\left(norootval_{2}-constantval\right)\left(norootval_{3}-constantval\right) \cdots\left(norootval_{infinitecount}-constantval\right)
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
norootval_{1} & endpointval & endpointval & endpointval & \cdots & endpointval & endpointval \\
startpoint & norootval_{2} & endpointval & endpointval & \cdots & endpointval & endpointval \\
startpoint & startpoint & norootval_{3} & endpointval & \cdots & endpointval & endpointval \\
startpoint & startpoint & startpoint & norootval_{4} & \cdots & endpointval & endpointval \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
startpoint & startpoint & startpoint & startpoint & \cdots & norootval_{infinitecount-1} & endpointval \\
startpoint & startpoint & startpoint & startpoint & \cdots & startpoint & norootval_{infinitecount}
\end{array}\right)=\frac{startpoint\, constantfn(endpointval)-endpointval\, constantfn(startpoint)}{startpoint-endpointval}
\]

The left side of (3) is a polynomial in $ startpoint $, and hence continuous in $ startpoint $. Letting $ endpointval=1 $, $ startpoint \rightarrow 1 $, and using L'Hopital's Rule, we find
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
norootval_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & norootval_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & norootval_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & norootval_{4} & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & norootval_{infinitecount-1} & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & norootval_{infinitecount}
\end{array}\right)=constantfn(1)-constantfn^{\prime}(1)
\]

Letting $ norootval_{fixedindex}=zeroparam_{fixedindex}+1 $, we recover (2), using for example equation (1) on page 136.

garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. Subtract the first row from each of the other rows, to get
\[
qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
-2 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
-2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & lksdjfhqo
\end{array}\right)
\]

Then for $ 2 \leq mhgtrploa \leq lksdjfhqo-1 $, add $ 2 /(mhgtrploa+1) $ times the $ mhgtrploa $th column to the first column to obtain
\[
qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
3+\frac{2}{3}+\frac{2}{4}+\cdots+\frac{2}{lksdjfhqo} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & 3 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 4 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 5 & \cdots & 0 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & lksdjfhqo
\end{array}\right) .
\]

The resulting matrix is upper triangular, so the determinant is the product of the diagonal elements, which is
\[
lksdjfhqo!\left(1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{lksdjfhqo}\right)
\]

Solution 2 (J.P. Grossman). We derive a recursion for $ qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo+1} $. Expand (1) along the last row to get $ qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}=lksdjfhqo\,qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo-1}+(lksdjfhqo-1)! $. Divide by $ lksdjfhqo! $ to obtain
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}}{lksdjfhqo!}=\frac{qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo-1}}{(lksdjfhqo-1)!}+\frac{1}{lksdjfhqo}
\]
from which the result follows.

Solution 3. Define
\[
qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo+1}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo}\right)=\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{cccccc}
1+plmoknija_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1+plmoknija_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1+plmoknija_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1+plmoknija_{4} & \cdots & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1+plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo}
\end{array}\right)
\]

The problem asks for $ qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}(2,3, \ldots, lksdjfhqo) $. We will prove the identity
\[
qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo+1}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo}\right)=\prod_{mhgtrploa=1}^{lksdjfhqo} plmoknija_{mhgtrploa}+\sum_{mhgtrploa=1}^{lksdjfhqo} \prod_{\substack{nvbcsplra=1 \\ nvbcsplra \neq mhgtrploa}}^{lksdjfhqo} plmoknija_{nvbcsplra}
\]

This formula follows immediately from the recursion
\[
qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo+1}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo}\right)=plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo}\,qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-1}\right)+plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-1}\,qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-2}, 0\right)
\]

To prove this recursion, subtract the $(lksdjfhqo-1)$st column from the $lksdjfhqo$th column, and then expand along the $lksdjfhqo$th column.

If all the $ plmoknija_{mhgtrploa} $ are nonzero, we can write the polynomial $ qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-1}\right) $ in the form
\[
qzxwvtnpa_{lksdjfhqo}\left(plmoknija_{1}, \ldots, plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-1}\right)=plmoknija_{1} plmoknija_{2} \cdots plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-1}\left(1+\frac{1}{plmoknija_{1}}+\frac{1}{plmoknija_{2}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{plmoknija_{lksdjfhqo-1}}\right)
\]

This problem is the special case $ plmoknija_{mhgtrploa}=mhgtrploa+1 $.

Remark. This formula can also be used for a generalization of Problem 1993B5; see page 188.

Solution 4. The following result is Problem 7 of Part VII of [PS], and appeared as Problem 1978A2 [PutnamII, p. 31].

Let $ zxcrtvbhu, qawsedrgy, uhbgtfrde_{1}, uhbgtfrde_{2}, \ldots, uhbgtfrde_{lksdjfhqo} $ be real numbers with $ zxcrtvbhu \neq qawsedrgy $. Define
\[
wsxedcrfv(sdkufnqwe)=\left(uhbgtfrde_{1}-sdkufnqwe\right)\left(uhbgtfrde_{2}-sdkufnqwe\right)\left(uhbgtfrde_{3}-sdkufnqwe\right) \cdots\left(uhbgtfrde_{lksdjfhqo}-sdkufnqwe\right)
\]

Then
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
uhbgtfrde_{1} & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu & \cdots & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu \\
qawsedrgy & uhbgtfrde_{2} & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu & \cdots & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu \\
qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & uhbgtfrde_{3} & zxcrtvbhu & \cdots & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu \\
qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & uhbgtfrde_{4} & \cdots & zxcrtvbhu & zxcrtvbhu \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & \cdots & uhbgtfrde_{lksdjfhqo-1} & zxcrtvbhu \\
qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & qawsedrgy & \cdots & qawsedrgy & uhbgtfrde_{lksdjfhqo}
\end{array}\right)=\frac{qawsedrgy\,wsxedcrfv(zxcrtvbhu)-zxcrtvbhu\,wsxedcrfv(qawsedrgy)}{qawsedrgy-zxcrtvbhu}
\]

The left side of (3) is a polynomial in $ qawsedrgy $, and hence continuous in $ qawsedrgy $. Letting $ zxcrtvbhu=1 $, $ qawsedrgy \rightarrow 1 $, and using L'Hopital's Rule, we find
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{ccccccc}
uhbgtfrde_{1} & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & uhbgtfrde_{2} & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & uhbgtfrde_{3} & 1 & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & uhbgtfrde_{4} & \cdots & 1 & 1 \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & uhbgtfrde_{lksdjfhqo-1} & 1 \\
1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 & uhbgtfrde_{lksdjfhqo}
\end{array}\right)=wsxedcrfv(1)-wsxedcrfv^{\prime}(1)
\]

Letting $ uhbgtfrde_{mhgtrploa}=plmoknija_{mhgtrploa}+1 $, we recover (2), using for example equation (1) on page 136.

kernel_variant.question:
For every integer $n\ge 3$ let  
\[
D_{n}=\operatorname{diag}(3,5,7,\dots ,2n+1),\qquad  
u=\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\\\vdots\\1\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbf R^{n},\qquad  
v=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\\vdots\\ n\end{pmatrix}\in\mathbf R^{n},
\]
and define the real symmetric matrix  
\[
A_{n}:=D_{n}+u u^{\mathsf T}+v v^{\mathsf T}.
\]

Put  
\[
\Delta_{n}:=\det A_{n}, \qquad S_{n}:=\frac{\Delta_{n}}{2^{\,n}n!}.
\]

Introduce the shorthand quantities  
\[
\sigma_{0}(n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{2k+1},\qquad
\sigma_{1}(n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k}{2k+1},\qquad
\sigma_{2}(n)=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{k^{2}}{2k+1}.
\]

(a)  Prove that  
\[
\boxed{\;
\Delta_{n}
=\Bigl(\prod_{k=1}^{n}(2k+1)\Bigr)\Bigl[\,(1+\sigma_{0}(n))(1+\sigma_{2}(n))-\sigma_{1}(n)^{2}\Bigr]\;.}
\tag{$\ast$}

(b)  Set  
\[
C_{n}:=\prod_{k=1}^{n}\Bigl(1+\frac{1}{2k}\Bigr)=\prod_{k=1}^{n}\frac{2k+1}{2k}.
\]
Show that  
\[
C_{n}=\sqrt{n}\,C\,(1+o(1)),\qquad n\to\infty ,
\]
with the universal constant  
\[
\boxed{\;C=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\;} \approx 1.12838.
\]

(c)  Using $(\ast)$ together with part (b) establish the asymptotic law  
\[
S_{n}=c\,n^{5/2}\ln n\,(1+o(1)),\qquad n\to\infty ,
\]
where  
\[
\boxed{\;c=\dfrac{1}{4\sqrt{\pi}}\;}\approx 0.14105.
\]

(d)  Deduce that the sequence $\bigl(S_{n}\bigr)_{n\ge 3}$ is unbounded and that $\Delta_{n}>0$ for all sufficiently large $n$.
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step 1.  Rank-two reduction.}  
Write $U=[\,u\;|\;v\,]\in\mathbf R^{n\times 2}$.  Since $A_{n}=D_{n}+UU^{\mathsf T}$ and $D_{n}$ is invertible, the matrix-determinant lemma gives  
\[
\Delta_{n}= \det(D_{n})\,\det\!\bigl(I_{2}+U^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}U\bigr).
\tag{1}
\]

\textbf{Step 2.  The $2\times 2$ core.}  
Because $D_{n}^{-1}=\operatorname{diag}\bigl(\tfrac{1}{2k+1}\bigr)_{k=1}^{n}$, we have  
\[
U^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}U=
\begin{pmatrix}u^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}u&u^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}v\\[2pt]
v^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}u&v^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}v\end{pmatrix}
=\begin{pmatrix}\sigma_{0}&\sigma_{1}\\[2pt]\sigma_{1}&\sigma_{2}\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Hence  
\[
\det\!\bigl(I_{2}+U^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}U\bigr)
=(1+\sigma_{0})(1+\sigma_{2})-\sigma_{1}^{2}.
\tag{2}
\]
Because $\det D_{n}=\prod_{k=1}^{n}(2k+1)$, (1)-(2) yield the identity $(\ast)$ required in part (a).

\textbf{Step 3.  Asymptotics of $\sigma_{0},\sigma_{1},\sigma_{2}$.}  
Use the elementary expansions  
\[
\frac{1}{2k+1}=\frac{1}{2k}\Bigl(1-\frac{1}{2k}+\mathrm O\!\bigl(k^{-2}\bigr)\Bigr),\qquad
\frac{k}{2k+1}=\frac12-\frac{1}{4k}+\mathrm O\!\bigl(k^{-2}\bigr),\qquad
\frac{k^{2}}{2k+1}=\frac{k}{2}-\frac14+\mathrm O\!\bigl(k^{-1}\bigr).
\]
Summation gives  
\[
\sigma_{1}(n)=\frac{n}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\ln n+\mathcal O(1),\qquad
\sigma_{2}(n)=\frac{n^{2}}{4}+\mathcal O(n).
\tag{3}
\]
For $\sigma_{0}(n)$ subtract the even terms of the harmonic series:
\[
\sigma_{0}(n)=H_{2n+1}-1-\frac12H_{n}
              =\frac12\ln n+\frac{\gamma}{2}+\ln 2-1+o(1),
\tag{4}
\]
where $H_{m}$ denotes the $m$-th harmonic number and $\gamma$ is Euler's constant.

\textbf{Step 4.  Size of the $2\times 2$ determinant.}  
Insert (3)-(4) into (2):
\[
(1+\sigma_{0})(1+\sigma_{2})-\sigma_{1}^{2}
=\sigma_{0}\sigma_{2}+O(n^{2})-\sigma_{1}^{2}
=\frac12\ln n\cdot\frac{n^{2}}{4}+O(n^{2})-\frac{n^{2}}{4}+O(n\ln n)
=\frac18\,n^{2}\ln n\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).
\tag{5}
\]

\textbf{Step 5.  Asymptotics of the odd double factorial.}  
The exact identity  
\[
(2n+1)!!=\frac{(2n+1)!}{2^{\,n}n!}
\]
combined with Stirling's formula,
\[
m!=\sqrt{2\pi m}\,m^{m}e^{-m}\bigl(1+\mathrm O(m^{-1})\bigr),
\qquad m\to\infty,
\]
yields  
\[
\begin{aligned}
(2n+1)!!
      &=\frac{(2n+1)\sqrt{4\pi n}\,(2n)^{2n}e^{-2n}}{2^{\,n}\sqrt{2\pi n}\,n^{\,n}e^{-n}}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr)\\
      &=\frac{(2n+1)}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,
        \frac{(2n)^{2n}}{2^{\,n}n^{\,n}}\,
        e^{-n}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr)\\
      &=\frac{(2n+1)}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,
        2^{\,n}n^{\,n}e^{-n}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr)\\
      &=2^{\,n}n!\,\sqrt{n}\,\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr).
\end{aligned}
\tag{6}
\]
Therefore  
\[
C_{n}:=\frac{(2n+1)!!}{2^{\,n}n!}
       =\sqrt{n}\,\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\Bigl(1+o(1)\Bigr),
\]
which is exactly the statement of part (b) with $C=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}$.

\textbf{Step 6.  Putting the pieces together.}  
Combine $(\ast)$, (5) and (6):
\[
\Delta_{n}=2^{\,n}n!\,C_{n}\cdot\frac18\,n^{2}\ln n\,(1+o(1))
          =2^{\,n}n!\,\sqrt{n}\,\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\cdot\frac18\,n^{2}\ln n\,(1+o(1))
          =2^{\,n}n!\,\frac{1}{4\sqrt{\pi}}\,n^{5/2}\ln n\,(1+o(1)).
\]
Dividing by $2^{\,n}n!$ we obtain  
\[
S_{n}=\frac{\Delta_{n}}{2^{\,n}n!}
      =\frac{1}{4\sqrt{\pi}}\,n^{5/2}\ln n\,(1+o(1)),
\]
which proves part (c) with $c=\dfrac{1}{4\sqrt{\pi}}>0$.

\textbf{Step 7.  Positivity and unboundedness.}  
Because $\ln n\to\infty$, the asymptotic formula in part (c) implies $S_{n}\to\infty$.  For all sufficiently large $n$, both the leading constant $c$ and $\ln n$ are positive, so $\Delta_{n}>0$ as well.  Hence $\bigl(S_{n}\bigr)$ is unbounded and eventually strictly positive, completing part (d).  

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Rank-2 Perturbation:  Unlike the original rank-1 (“all-ones”) perturbation, Aₙ is the sum of a diagonal matrix and TWO independent rank-1 matrices.  One must invoke the full 2×2 version of the matrix-determinant lemma; elementary row/column operations no longer suffice.

•  Multiple Nested Sums:  Evaluation of the 2×2 determinant requires the simultaneous control of σ₀, σ₁, σ₂, mixing harmonic, linear-growth, and quadratic-growth sums.

•  Asymptotic Analysis:  After the exact reduction, one must still perform a careful asymptotic expansion (Stirling’s formula, integral comparison) to obtain the growth rate.

•  Sign Determination:  The problem asks not only for boundedness but also the eventual sign and precise order of growth, necessitating finer estimates than a simple bounded/unbounded dichotomy.

•  Interaction of Concepts:  Low-rank perturbations, determinant identities, asymptotic series, and factorial estimates all interact.  No single “pattern-matching” trick solves the problem; the solver must orchestrate several advanced techniques, making this variant substantially harder than both the original statement and the intermediate kernel version.

For every integer $n\ge 3$ let  
\[

(b)  Set

\textbf{Step 2.  The $2\times 2$ core.}

\[
U^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}U=
\begin{pmatrix}u^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}u&u^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}v\\[2pt]
v^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}u&v^{\mathsf T}D_{n}^{-1}v\end{pmatrix}

\]

\[

\[

\tag{5}
\]

The exact identity  
\[
(2n+1)!!=\frac{(2n+1)!}{2^{\,n}n!}
\]

\[
m!=\sqrt{2\pi m}\,m^{m}e^{-m}\bigl(1+\mathrm O(m^{-1})\bigr),
\qquad m\to\infty,
\]
yields  
\[
\begin{aligned}
(2n+1)!!
      &=\frac{(2n+1)\sqrt{4\pi n}\,(2n)^{2n}e^{-2n}}{2^{\,n}\sqrt{2\pi n}\,n^{\,n}e^{-n}}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr)\\
      &=\frac{(2n+1)}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,
        \frac{(2n)^{2n}}{2^{\,n}n^{\,n}}\,
        e^{-n}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr)\\
      &=\frac{(2n+1)}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,
        2^{\,n}n^{\,n}e^{-n}\Bigl(1+\mathrm O\!\bigl(n^{-1}\bigr)\Bigr)\\

\[
